sections.h: Why you can't just run the original WOLF3D.EXE
sections.html:
This is the single most important thing to understand. The original Wolfenstein 3D is a 16-bit DOS real-mode program — there was never an official Windows (Win32) version. ExeBrowser's Wine layer (Wine 1.7.55 on the Boxedwine x86 emulator) is built to run Win32 executables, not DOS ones. Wine's DOS support is thin and unreliable, so dropping in the raw WOLF3D.EXE will most likely refuse to start or misbehave.

The clean, fully-legal workaround is a source port: after id open-sourced the Wolfenstein 3D engine in 1995, the community built modern rebuilds — ECWolf and Wolf4SDL — that compile to native Windows EXEs and read the very same shareware .WL1 game data. That's a real Win32 program Wine can actually load.

sections.h: What to expect — and the caveats
sections.html:
When it runs, you get the genuine 1992 game: B.J. Blazkowicz, the maze-like Nazi bunker, secret-wall pushing, and that unmistakable raycast look. The honest caveats:

  • Source ports use SDL2. ECWolf depends on SDL2/SDL2_mixer and targets Windows XP and up — heavier than DOOM's tiny Win32 EXE, so it's less of a sure thing under this older Wine. If ECWolf stalls, try the leaner Wolf4SDL, or switch the runtime to the r18 engine variant.
  • Sound is the flaky part. Music and digitized sound effects route through SDL audio under Wine, which is the most fragile path here. The video and controls are far more reliable than the audio.
  • Speed. Wolfenstein 3D is featherweight by modern standards, so even at the emulator's 10–40% of native it generally plays at full speed on a current laptop. Older Chromebooks and tablets may chug.

Net result: free, legal, and genuinely playable through a source port — but with enough setup and audio uncertainty that we rate it partial rather than a one-click classic.

sections.h: Step by step
sections.html:
1. Download the shareware data (the *.WL1 files) and unzip them on your computer.
  2. Download a 32-bit Windows source port (ECWolf or Wolf4SDL) and unzip it into the same folder as the .WL1 files.
  3. Click ▶ Play Wolfenstein 3D above to boot Wine on this page.
  4. Use Load your own copy → a folder and pick that combined folder.
  5. Select the source-port EXE (e.g. ECWOLF.EXE) as the entry and click Run in Wine.
  6. Click the canvas to capture input. Arrows move, Ctrl fires, Space opens/pushes walls, 1–4 switch weapons, Esc for the menu and to release the mouse.

The registered episodes, the Nocturnal Missions, and Spear of Destiny are commercial and are not included — buy those from id or Steam.

faq.q: Why can't I just run the original WOLF3D.EXE?
faq.a: Because it's a 16-bit DOS program and there was never an official Windows version. ExeBrowser's Wine layer runs Win32 executables, and Wine's DOS support is too thin to rely on. The fix is an open-source source port (ECWolf / Wolf4SDL) that is a real Win32 EXE and reads the same shareware .WL1 data.

faq.a: It's the real game data — the actual 1992 maps, sprites, and sound from the shareware episode — played through an open-source rebuild of id's engine that id themselves released to the public. It's not a fan re-creation of the levels; it's the original content, run by a modern, faithful engine.

faq.q: The sound isn't working or it won't start. What can I try?
faq.a: Audio through SDL under this older Wine is the most fragile part — video and controls usually hold up even when sound doesn't. If the port won't launch at all, try the leaner Wolf4SDL instead of ECWolf, or switch the runtime to the r18 engine variant. Make sure the EXE and all the *.WL1 files are in the same folder.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html:
There are two layers to be honest about here: the license and the binary.

The license is the easy part. Author Jason Emery released the complete Tyrian 2000 as freeware in 2004, and the artwork was later placed under an open-content license. The data files (graphics, levels, music) are freely redistributable, and the game is also offered free of charge on GOG. So there's nothing shady about playing it — no piracy, no cracks, no commercial copy required.

The binary is the tricky part. The original TYRIAN.EXE / TYRIAN2K.EXE is a DOS executable. ExeBrowser's Wine targets 32-bit Win32, so a pure DOS binary will not start cleanly without a DOS layer. That's why the verdict here is partial: the game is free and the gameplay is timeless, but getting it running in a browser Win32 environment takes the right approach.

sections.h: The reliable path: the OpenTyrian2000 port
sections.html:
The cleanest way to run Tyrian 2000 in ExeBrowser is the open-source OpenTyrian2000 source port — a faithful, native rebuild of the game that ships as a real Win32 executable rather than a DOS one. You load the port's .exe alongside the freeware Tyrian 2000 data files and it plays the original game, levels and all.

  • Grab an OpenTyrian2000 Windows release (it's GPL-licensed and free).
  • Grab the freeware Tyrian 2000 data (the tyrian2000.zip set of .dat, .shp, .lvl files).
  • Put the data files in a data\ folder right next to the port's EXE, then load the whole folder above.

Honesty note: OpenTyrian2000 is SDL-based, and SDL plus the Wine + WebAssembly layers can be demanding — expect it to run, but a heavy SDL2 build may be slower or fussier than a lightweight SDL1 one. If one build stutters, try another release, or the r18 variant if the default crashes on launch.

sections.h: Controls & tips
sections.html:
Click the game canvas to capture input, then:

  • ↑↓←→ or the mouse to fly your ship
  • Ctrl / Space to fire (and the secondary fire key for your sidekicks)
  • Enter / Esc to navigate menus; Esc also releases the mouse back to the page

Tyrian's whole charm is the upgrade shop between levels — spend your credits on weapons, shields and a beefier hull. Don't blow it all on guns; survivability wins the long episodes.

faq.q: Is Tyrian 2000 really free to play?
faq.a: Yes. The full game was released as freeware by the rights holder in 2004, the graphics were later put under an open-content license, and it's also given away free on GOG. There's nothing to buy and nothing shady about playing it.

faq.q: Can I play Tyrian 2000 in the browser with no download?
faq.a: Sort of — you play it in your browser via Wine + WebAssembly, with no install, but you do need to load the game files into the player. Because Tyrian is a DOS game, the smoothest route is the open-source OpenTyrian2000 Win32 port plus the freeware data files, loaded with the on-page uploader.

faq.q: Why doesn't the original Tyrian EXE just run?
faq.a: The original Tyrian / Tyrian 2000 executable is a DOS program. ExeBrowser's Wine targets 32-bit Win32, so a pure DOS binary won't start cleanly without a DOS layer. The OpenTyrian2000 source port is a native Win32 rebuild, which is why it's the recommended way to play here.

faq.q: What is OpenTyrian2000?
faq.a: It's a free, open-source (GPL) port of Tyrian 2000 that recreates the original game as a modern, native executable using the freeware Tyrian 2000 data files. It's the most reliable way to run the game inside ExeBrowser's Win32 environment.

faq.q: Will it run at full speed?
faq.a: Maybe. ExeBrowser emulates an x86 CPU inside WebAssembly at roughly 10-40% of native speed, and OpenTyrian2000 is SDL-based, which adds overhead. The game is light enough that a modern laptop often handles it fine, but expect possible slowdown on older tablets or Chromebooks.

intro: Freedoom is a fully free, open-source replacement for the DOOM data files — same legendary gameplay, zero proprietary content. It runs with the classic DOS DOOM.EXE engine in DOSBox + WebAssembly. Hit play for instant browser-based demon slaying, no download required.

download.heading: Get Freedoom (free & open source)
download.html:
The official, libre release

Freedoom's game data is released under a modified BSD license — you may freely copy, modify, and redistribute it, provided the license and credit travel with it. Grab the official release from the project itself:

Download Freedoom (official site) →

The download contains freedoom1.wad (Phase 1, Ultimate-Doom-style) and freedoom2.wad (Phase 2, Doom-II-style). These are WAD data files, not an executable — you also need a DOOM engine. The simplest free pairing is a small Win32 software-rendering port such as Chocolate Doom or Crispy Doom. Put the port's .EXE and a freedoomN.wad in one folder, then use “Load your own copy” above to drop the folder in. Everything here — game and engine — is genuinely free and open source.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html: ExeBrowser runs a real Win32 executable on Wine + WebAssembly. Freedoom itself isn't an executable — it's an IWAD, a single data file holding all the levels, graphics, sound, and music. So the page actually runs a DOOM engine (a small Win32 .exe) and points it at freedoom1.wad or freedoom2.wad. Pick a software-rendering port and it plays at full speed on a modern laptop, exactly like the original DOOM does here.

sections.h: Choose the right engine — this is the important part
sections.html:
The engine choice decides whether it works. Be guided by this:

  • Chocolate Doom / Crispy Doom (recommended) — software-rendering, tiny Win32 builds. No 3D acceleration needed, so they run cleanly in the emulator. This is the reliable pairing.
  • GZDoom / modern hardware ports (avoid here) — these require OpenGL 3.3 and a real GPU pipeline that Boxedwine doesn't provide. They will fail to start or render. Save those for a native desktop.

To launch, put the port's .EXE and a Freedoom WAD in the same folder. Many ports auto-detect a WAD sitting beside them; if not, the engine reads it as the IWAD on startup. Phase 1 (freedoom1.wad) is the four-episode, Ultimate-Doom-style campaign; Phase 2 (freedoom2.wad) is a single 30-level Doom-II-style campaign.

sections.h: Step by step
sections.html:
1. Click ▶ Play Freedoom above to boot Wine on this page.
  2. Download Freedoom from the box below and a small software-rendering port (Chocolate Doom or Crispy Doom).
  3. Unzip both into a single folder so the .EXE and a freedoomN.wad sit side by side.
  4. Use Load your own copy → a folder and select that folder, then click Run in Wine.
  5. Click the canvas to capture input. Arrows move, Ctrl fires, Space opens doors, 1–7 switch weapons, Esc releases the mouse.

sections.h: What it is — and what it isn't
sections.html: Freedoom is not id Software's DOOM and isn't trying to be a copy of its art — it's an original, drop-in replacement for the commercial WADs, made from scratch by volunteers so the open-source engines have something completely free to play. That makes it perfect for a browser: no shareware caveats, no licensing tightrope, no "buy the full version" upsell. If you specifically want the original 1993 DOOM levels, see our DOOM shareware page instead. If you just want a free, no-strings DOOM-style shooter to blast through, Freedoom is the cleanest option there is.

faq.q: Is Freedoom really free and legal to play online?
faq.a: Yes — completely. Freedoom is released under a modified BSD license that explicitly allows anyone to copy, modify, and redistribute it freely. It contains no id Software assets, so there are no shareware or copyright caveats. Both the game data and the open-source engines you pair it with are free software.

faq.q: Is Freedoom the same as DOOM?
faq.a: No. Freedoom is an original, from-scratch game that runs on the classic DOOM engine and is compatible with DOOM-format levels and mods. It's a free replacement for id's commercial DOOM WADs — similar feel, all-original content. If you want the actual 1993 DOOM levels, see our DOOM shareware page.

faq.q: Why do I need a separate engine to play Freedoom?
faq.a: Freedoom ships as WAD data files (freedoom1.wad and freedoom2.wad), not a program. A DOOM engine reads that data and runs the game. In the browser, use a lightweight software-rendering port like Chocolate Doom or Crispy Doom — both are small, free Win32 builds that run well on Wine + WebAssembly.

faq.q: Can I use GZDoom for Freedoom in the browser?
faq.a: Not here. GZDoom and other modern hardware-accelerated ports require OpenGL 3.3, which the WebAssembly emulator doesn't provide, so they won't render. Stick to software-rendering ports (Chocolate Doom, Crispy Doom) in the browser, and save GZDoom for a native desktop install.

faq.q: What's the difference between Freedoom Phase 1 and Phase 2?
faq.a: Phase 1 (freedoom1.wad) is built like The Ultimate Doom — four episodes of eight levels each — and works with mods made for the original DOOM. Phase 2 (freedoom2.wad) is a single 30-level campaign in the style of DOOM II and works with DOOM II / Final Doom mods.

intro: 3D Pinball for Windows – Space Cadet is one of the most-missed pieces of bundled Windows software ever — the single pinball table that shipped with Windows 95 Plus!, 98, 2000, and XP before quietly disappearing in Vista. The good news: it's a small, well-behaved 32-bit Win32 program, and it runs in ExeBrowser without much fuss. Pick the Win32 + Gecko variant, upload the whole pinball folder, point Wine at PINBALL.EXE, and you're launching the ball in seconds — full speed, working sound, nothing uploaded to a server.

sections.h: Why it needs the whole folder
sections.html: Space Cadet isn't a single self-contained EXE. PINBALL.EXE loads PINBALL.DAT (the table graphics and physics data), PINBALL.MID and WAVE*.WAV for sound, and table-string files from the same directory. If you drop only the EXE, you'll get a window that complains it can't find its table — or simply shows nothing. Upload the entire folder using the folder picker so those files sit beside the executable inside Wine's virtual C:\.

sections.h: Why the +Gecko variant

intro: Age of Empires is the test case people most want answered: can a real RTS classic run in a browser tab? The honest answer is sometimes, and not at full speed. The original 1997 Age of Empires and Age of Empires II: The Age of Kings are 32-bit Win32 games from the right era, so they're plausible — but they lean on DirectDraw and DirectSound and are far more CPU-hungry than a card game, which is where browser-based emulation shows its limits. Expect to revisit a campaign mission or a small skirmish, not run a competitive late-game match.

sections.h: What to expect from performance
sections.html:
You're emulating an x86 CPU inside WebAssembly, which delivers roughly 10–40% of native speed. The original AoE games ran fine on a late-90s Pentium, so a fast modern laptop has headroom — but a busy late-game map with hundreds of units doing pathfinding will feel sluggish, and audio can stutter. Small skirmishes, the campaign tutorial, and menus are the most playable. This is a "revisit it / show someone" experience, not a competitive-multiplayer one.

AppsAge of Empires (1997), Age of Empires II: The Age of Kings (1999)
Best Wine variantTry Win32 (default) first, then the older r18 engine if it crashes on boot
Graphics2D DirectDraw — within reach; the 3D HD/Definitive remasters are not
Expected resultMay launch and play, but slowly; menus and small maps fare best

sections.h: Load the full install folder, not just the EXE
sections.html: AoE is not a single EXE — it needs its data, graphics, and sound files (the data, sound, and campaign folders plus various DLLs). Use the pick a folder or pick a zip option and upload the entire installed game directory so the executable finds everything beside it. Dropping just EMPIRES.EXE or AGE2_X1.EXE on its own will fail immediately.

sections.h: If it won't start
sections.html:
• Crash on launch? Try the r18 variant — its older engine sometimes starts games the default chokes on.
  • Black screen? Open the Console and look for err: lines about a missing DLL, then add that file to your folder.
  • Wrong resolution / tiny window? Some builds accept a windowed or resolution flag; the data-folder version is most forgiving.
  • The HD / Definitive editions won't work — they're modern 64-bit Direct3D titles outside what this engine supports. Use an original 1997/1999 disc-era install instead.

faq.q: Can I really play Age of Empires in a browser?
faq.a: Sometimes. The original 1997 Age of Empires and AoE II (1999) are 32-bit Win32 games that can launch under Boxedwine, but they rely on DirectDraw and are CPU-heavy, so expect reduced speed and occasional audio stutter. Small skirmishes and campaign missions play best; large late-game battles will feel sluggish.

faq.q: Why won't the HD or Definitive Edition run?
faq.a: The HD and Definitive Editions are modern 64-bit games built on Direct3D. ExeBrowser only runs 32-bit Win32 software without DirectX 9+, so those remasters are outside what the engine supports. Use an original disc-era install of the 1997 or 1999 game.

faq.q: Do you host Age of Empires for me to download?
faq.a: No. Age of Empires is commercial software owned by Microsoft and we don't redistribute it. Bring your own legally-owned copy, or buy the official editions from ageofempires.com. We only host games that are free to redistribute.

faq.q: Why do I need to upload the whole folder instead of just the EXE?
faq.a: Age of Empires reads its data, sound, and campaign files from folders next to the executable. If you upload only EMPIRES.EXE or AGE2_X1.EXE, it can't find those assets and crashes on launch. Always upload the entire installed game directory as a folder or zip.

faq.q: It crashes the moment it boots — what can I try?
faq.a: Switch to the r18 Wine variant; its older engine sometimes starts games the default build chokes on. If you still get a black screen, open the Console and look for err: lines naming a missing DLL, then add that file to your uploaded folder.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect

SkiFree is about as friendly as a Windows program gets for ExeBrowser. The 32-bit version (1.04) that Chris Pirih recompiled in 2005 is a small Win32 executable that renders with ordinary GDI graphics — no DirectX, no 3D acceleration, no .NET, no online features. That's exactly the profile that runs well under Boxedwine, and the author himself notes the build "should also work on Wine." Expect smooth, full-speed skiing on any modern laptop.

sections.h: The controls (and the yeti)
sections.html:
Click the game canvas first so it captures your input, then:

  • ↓ or F — speed up / fast mode (your only hope against the yeti)
  • ← → — steer; hold to carve harder
  • ↑ — slow down / face uphill
  • Space / number keys — tricks off jumps in freestyle
  • You can also steer with the mouse — the skier follows the pointer
  • Esc — release the mouse back to the page

Ski down the endless slope avoiding trees, stumps, rocks and the little dogs. Clear a course (slalom, tree slalom, or freestyle) and you reach the free-skiing stretch — where, at 2000 metres, the Abominable Snowman charges in to gobble you up. Tap into fast mode and you can outrun him for a while; the course loops and he'll hound you for the rest of your run. Outlasting the yeti is the whole legend.

intro: JezzBall — the maddening 1992 puzzle game from the Microsoft Entertainment Pack where you wall off bouncing balls into smaller and smaller boxes — is exactly the kind of light, mouse-driven 16-bit title that ExeBrowser's Wine + WebAssembly runtime handles well. There's no DirectX, no 3D, no networking; just a single window, a mouse pointer, and balls to trap. The one real catch: JezzBall is commercial Microsoft software and we can't give you the files, so you'll need to load a copy you already legally own. Here's how to run it, which Wine variant to pick, and a few free clones if you'd rather skip the licensing hassle.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html:
JezzBall is a 16-bit Windows 3.x program from 1992. That matters here: the everyday default Win32 Wine variant is built for 32-bit apps and usually won't load a 16-bit executable. ExeBrowser ships a dedicated win3x variant for exactly this era of software, and that's the one this page is configured to use. When you load your own JEZZBALL.EXE (or the WEP launcher it came with), pick the win3x runtime.

Mechanically the game is trivial for the emulator: a small playfield, a couple of bouncing balls, and walls that grow when you click. No GPU, no sound-card gymnastics required. If your legally-owned copy starts at all, it generally plays at full, responsive speed — this is about as gentle a workload as Wine-in-the-browser ever sees.

Honest expectation: the blocker is almost never performance — it's getting a legitimate copy in the first place, since Microsoft no longer sells the Entertainment Pack. That's why we've set this page's verdict to partial: the technology works, but you have to bring your own files.

sections.h: The gameplay, in 20 seconds
sections.html:
You're given a rectangular room with one or more balls bouncing around it. Click to start building a wall — it grows in both directions from your pointer (vertical or horizontal, toggled with a right-click or the mouse button). When a wall reaches the edges on both ends, it seals off that section; if the sealed-off part contains no ball, it fills in and shrinks the open area.

  • Clear 75% or more of the playfield to finish a level.
  • If a ball hits a wall while it's still growing, you lose a life.
  • Each new level adds another ball and ramps up the speed.

It's deceptively simple and genuinely tense — squeezing the last ball into a sliver of the board is the whole appeal.

sections.h: Loading your own copy
sections.html:
1. Click ▶ Play JezzBall above to boot the win3x Wine runtime in this tab.
  2. Use Load your own copy → a folder and select the directory from your legally-owned Entertainment Pack that contains JEZZBALL.EXE.
  3. Make sure the runtime is set to win3x (this page defaults to it) — the 16-bit binary won't start under the default Win32 variant.
  4. If the bundled launcher (WEP.EXE / a pack menu) doesn't cooperate, point the runtime straight at JEZZBALL.EXE instead.
  5. Once the window appears, play with the mouse — click to lay walls, the right button flips wall orientation.

If win3x crashes or hangs on launch, try the r18 variant — it uses an older Boxedwine engine that sometimes handles stubborn 16-bit programs differently.

sections.h: Prefer something free? Try an open-source clone
sections.html:
Because the original is proprietary and hard to obtain legally, many players just grab a faithful open-source remake — no licensing grey area, and these run natively on modern systems:

  • KBounce — the KDE clone of JezzBall, free and open-source.
  • IceBreaker — a GPL JezzBall-style game (you trap penguins instead of balls).

These aren't Windows .exe files we run here, but they're the clean, legal way to scratch the JezzBall itch if you don't own the original Entertainment Pack.

faq.q: Can I play JezzBall online for free with no download?
faq.a: You can play it in your browser with no install, but JezzBall itself is commercial Microsoft software that we don't host. You'd need to load a copy you legally own. If you want a truly free option, an open-source clone like KBounce or IceBreaker gives you the same gameplay with no licensing concerns.

faq.q: Why won't JezzBall run on the default Wine variant?
faq.a: JezzBall is a 16-bit Windows 3.x program from 1992. The default ExeBrowser runtime targets 32-bit Win32 apps and generally can't load 16-bit executables. Use the win3x variant (this page is set to it by default), which is built specifically for early-1990s Windows software.

faq.q: Is JezzBall legal to download from abandonware sites?
faq.a: It's a legal grey area we steer clear of. Microsoft has never released JezzBall as freeware and no longer sells the Entertainment Pack, so abandonware copies aren't authorized redistribution. ExeBrowser won't link to them. Load only a copy you legally own, or play a free open-source clone instead.

faq.q: Will JezzBall run at full speed in the browser?
faq.a: Almost certainly, if your owned copy starts at all. It's a tiny 2D game with no 3D, DirectX, or sound-card demands, so it's one of the lightest possible loads for the emulator. The hard part is obtaining a legitimate copy, not performance.

faq.q: What are the controls for JezzBall?
faq.a: It's mouse-driven. Click to start growing a wall from your pointer (it extends in both directions); the right mouse button toggles between vertical and horizontal walls. Don't let a bouncing ball touch a wall while it's still building, or you lose a life.

intro: Chip's Challenge — the deviously good top-down tile puzzler that hooked a generation through the Microsoft Entertainment Pack — running in a browser tab. Guide Chip McCallahan through 149 grid mazes: collect computer chips, dodge fireballs and walkers, slide across ice and force-floors, and reach the exit to advance. The classic CHIPS.EXE is a 16-bit Windows 3.1 program, which is exactly what ExeBrowser's win3x runtime is built for. It's an honest partial: it boots and plays, but 16-bit-on-Wine has rough edges, and you'll need to bring your own legally-obtained copy — we can't host this one.

sections.h: Which Chip's Challenge is this — and why it runs at all
sections.html:
There are really two PC versions, and the distinction matters here:

  • The Microsoft Entertainment Pack build (CHIPS.EXE, 1992) — the one most people remember from Windows 3.1 / 95. It's a tiny 16-bit Windows program that draws with plain GDI: no DirectX, no 3D, no networking. That simplicity is why it's a realistic target for ExeBrowser. Modern 64-bit Windows can't even run it anymore (Microsoft dropped 16-bit support), so a Wine layer is one of the few ways to play the original.
  • The 2015 Niffler re-release (on Steam) — a modernized 32-bit rebuild. It's the version that's now free-to-keep on Steam, but it's a newer build with its own runtime expectations and is better played natively on a real PC.

ExeBrowser targets the 16-bit classic, loaded through the win3x variant — our 16-bit Windows 3.x mode, made for early-1990s apps exactly like this.

sections.h: What to expect (the honest version)
sections.html:
Because Chip's Challenge is a turn-based 2D puzzle with no fancy graphics, raw speed isn't a problem — the puzzles don't care that an x86 CPU is being emulated in WebAssembly. The friction is the 16-bit-on-Wine layer:

  • Boots and plays: the board renders, arrow-key movement works, and the level logic is intact. As a puzzle game it's very playable.
  • Rough edges: 16-bit apps under Wine can have palette/redraw glitches, finicky menus, or the occasional refusal to launch. If it won't start cleanly on the first try, that's expected with this era of software.
  • Sound is hit-or-miss: the old MME/PC-speaker audio path may be silent. The game is fully playable muted.
  • No save guarantees: high scores and progress are written to .ini/registry-style files that may not persist between browser sessions. Treat a run as a single sitting.

That's why the verdict is partial, not works great — it's a genuine, playable port of a 30-year-old 16-bit binary, warts and all.

sections.h: Controls & how to load it
sections.html:
Controls are simple and keyboard-driven:

  • ↑ ↓ ← → — move Chip one tile in that direction
  • Click the game canvas first to capture keyboard input; Esc releases it back to the page

intro: Rodent's Revenge is the addictive little puzzle game from Microsoft Entertainment Pack 2 (1991): you're a mouse pushing blocks to corner the cats, who turn into cheese when they're trapped. sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html:
Rodent's Revenge is about as gentle a workload as Wine gets. There's no hardware rendering, no sound mixing to speak of, and the whole game is a grid of sprites that update a few cells at a time. On ExeBrowser's emulated x86 CPU you'll still get roughly 10–40% of native speed, but for a turn-based block-pusher that's effectively instant — the bottleneck is you deciding where to shove the next block, not the emulator.

Expect the original tiny window (it was designed for 640×480 Windows 3.1 desktops), crisp pixel graphics, and the familiar squeak-and-trap loop across its 50 levels. It will look exactly like 1991, because it is 1991 — this runs the real executable, not a remake.

sections.h: Why you need the win3x runtime
sections.html:
This is the important bit. Rodent's Revenge is a 16-bit Windows 3.x application. Modern 64-bit Windows can't run it at all, and even ExeBrowser's everyday default (Win32) runtime won't load a 16-bit binary. ExeBrowser provides a dedicated win3x runtime for exactly these 1990–1994 programs, and that's what this page is set to use.

  • If the game refuses to start on the default engine, that's expected — make sure you're on the win3x variant (this page selects it for you).
  • The original package often needs a couple of helper DLLs alongside the EXE — commonly VBRUN100.DLL and WEPUTIL.DLL. If your copy includes them, keep the whole folder together when you load it.
  • Load the folder, not just the EXE, so Wine can find those DLLs and any data files next to it.

verdict.text: Works well — minimalist, no DirectX needed

intro: Chocolate Doom is the source port built for one purpose: to play exactly like the 1993 original, bugs and all. It's deliberately minimalist — no fancy 3D, no DirectX 9, no launcher bloat — which is precisely why it runs cleanly here. The real Win32 build executes on Wine + WebAssembly right in this tab, loading the freely-redistributable shareware DOOM1.WAD. No download, no install, no account. Hit play and you're in Knee-Deep in the Dead with pixel-faithful, vanilla Doom.

sections.h: Why Chocolate Doom runs so well here
sections.html:
Most modern Doom source ports (GZDoom and friends) lean on OpenGL or DirectX hardware acceleration — exactly the things 32-bit Wine 1.7.55 inside an emulator handles worst. Chocolate Doom goes the other way on purpose. It renders the original 320×200 software framebuffer through SDL and can fall back to plain Windows GDI, with no demand for a GPU or shader pipeline. That low-tech approach is what makes it one of the friendlier games for ExeBrowser: the heavy lifting is just CPU drawing a tiny frame, which the WebAssembly x86 emulator can keep up with comfortably.

It's a 32-bit Win32 executable with no 64-bit dependency, no .NET, and no DRM, so it sits squarely inside what this environment supports.

sections.h: Which build to load (this matters)
sections.html:
Chocolate Doom comes in two lineages, and the older one is the safer bet in the browser:

  • 2.3.0 (SDL 1.2) — the last release before the SDL2 rewrite. It does all its drawing in software with the simplest possible video path, which tends to be the most reliable under Boxedwine. Start here.
  • 3.0 and newer (SDL2) — these moved screen scaling into hardware for crisp high-resolution windows. That's great natively but asks more of the emulated graphics layer, so it can be hit-or-miss in a tab. Try it only if the 2.x build misbehaves.

Whichever you load, drop the source-port EXE and DOOM1.WAD into the same folder so the engine finds its game data.

sections.h: What to expect — honestly
sections.html:
Video: the game itself is featherweight, so the picture should be smooth on a modern laptop. Older Chromebooks and tablets emulate x86 more slowly — playable, but don't expect a perfectly locked frame rate.

Audio: sound is the usual wildcard under Boxedwine. Music and effects may work, stutter, or stay silent depending on your browser and the build; the gameplay is unaffected if audio drops out. The leaner 2.3.0 build is generally the better-behaved one.

Setup screen: Chocolate Doom ships a separate chocolate-setup.exe for key bindings and options. The game runs fine with defaults, so you can skip it — but if you do open it, choose the software/GDI video options rather than anything labeled fullscreen-accelerated.

faq.q: Is Chocolate Doom free and legal to play here?
faq.a: Yes. Chocolate Doom itself is open-source under the GNU GPL v2, so it can be freely redistributed. The game data we load is DOOM1.WAD — the first episode, Knee-Deep in the Dead, released as free shareware in 1993 and still freely redistributable. The registered episodes and Doom II are commercial; buy those from id / Steam and load your own WAD.

faq.q: What's the difference between Chocolate Doom and just running DOOM?
faq.a: Chocolate Doom is a modern recompilation of id's open-sourced engine that intentionally preserves vanilla behavior — same 320×200 look, same limits, same quirks as the 1993 game — while being a clean 32-bit Win32 EXE that's easy to run on today's systems. Plain DOS DOOM.EXE can be fussy under Wine; Chocolate Doom sidesteps that and is the closest thing to original-accurate Doom that runs reliably in a browser tab.

faq.q: Does Chocolate Doom need DirectX or a GPU to run?
faq.a: No. It draws in software through SDL and can use plain Windows GDI, so it needs neither DirectX 9+ nor a graphics card. That's exactly why it runs better here than heavier ports like GZDoom, which rely on hardware acceleration that 32-bit Wine on WebAssembly handles poorly.

faq.q: Why is there no sound, or why does it stutter?
faq.a: Audio is the least reliable part of emulating an x86 Win32 app inside WebAssembly. Music and sound effects may work, crackle, or stay silent depending on your browser and the build you load. It doesn't affect gameplay. The older 2.3.0 (SDL 1.2) build tends to behave best — try that first.

faq.q: Can I play deathmatch or co-op online here?
faq.a: Not in the browser. Chocolate Doom's networking expects real UDP sockets, which this emulated environment doesn't provide. Single-player and loading the shareware episode work; multiplayer netplay does not.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html: ExeBrowser boots a real copy of Wine on top of a 32-bit x86 emulator compiled to WebAssembly, then runs an actual Windows .exe. That's perfect for Win32 software — but the 1990 Commander Keen was written for MS-DOS, in 16-bit real mode, talking directly to EGA video and the PC speaker. Wine targets Win32, and the Wine 1.7.55 engine here has only thin DOS-machine support (older Wine historically shelled out to DOSBox for DOS programs, a path that isn't reliable in this WebAssembly build). So if you drop the raw KEEN1.EXE in and hit run, it will most likely refuse to start or exit immediately. That's expected, and it's why this page is marked partial rather than a green “just works.”

Commander Genius is the best-known, an open-source reimplementation that plays the real Marooned on Mars levels. Because a source port is a Win32 EXE, it runs in ExeBrowser the way DOOM source ports do.

verdict.text: Won't run here — it's a DOS game, not Windows

intro: Straight answer first: Scorched Earth will not run on ExeBrowser, and we'd rather tell you that up front than waste your time. The legendary "Mother of All Games" — Wendell Hicken's 1991 tank-artillery classic where you lob nukes and Death's Heads across destructible terrain — is a 16-bit MS-DOS program (SCORCH.EXE). ExeBrowser runs Windows .exe files through Wine, and Wine doesn't execute raw DOS binaries — that's a job for DOSBox. So there's no play button here that would actually work. Below is the honest why, where to grab the author's free copy, and the route that does get you playing Scorched Earth in a browser today.

sections.h: Why it won't run in ExeBrowser
sections.html:
ExeBrowser is Boxedwine — Wine 1.7.55 plus a 32-bit x86 emulator compiled to WebAssembly. It runs Windows programs: Win32 executables, and to a limited degree old 16-bit Windows 3.x apps. Scorched Earth is none of those. It is a pure MS-DOS game, written in Borland C++ and Turbo Assembler in 1991 to talk directly to DOS and a VGA card. There was never a Windows version.

Wine famously "is not an emulator" — it translates Windows API calls. It has no DOS layer of its own; when handed a DOS executable, real Wine simply hands it off to DOSBox. Inside ExeBrowser there is no DOSBox to hand off to, so SCORCH.EXE just won't start. This isn't a slowness or compatibility quirk we can flag as "partial" — it's the wrong kind of executable for this engine entirely. That's why the verdict here is a clean no.

sections.h: How to actually play Scorched Earth in a browser (DOSBox, not Wine)
sections.html:
The good news: Scorched Earth plays beautifully in a browser — just through a DOS emulator instead of Wine. Several sites wrap the game in js-dos / DOSBox compiled to WebAssembly, which is the correct tool for a DOS title:

  • dos.zone — Scorched Earth — instant in-browser DOSBox play.
  • retrogames.cz — Scorched Earth (DOS) — another browser-based DOSBox build.

Prefer to run it yourself? Download version 1.5 from the author and run it under desktop DOSBox (or DOSBox-X). Mount the folder, type SCORCH, and you're in the menu.

sections.h: Where to get the game — and pay the author if you can
sections.html:
Scorched Earth started as classic $10-by-mail shareware. Today its creator, Wendell Hicken, posts the final release (v1.5, scorch15.zip) for free on his own site, with a pay-what-you-want option and a "Totally Scorched" bundle of older versions:

Get Scorched Earth 1.5 from the author →

This is the legitimate source. The game is free to download there, and if you loved it back in the day, the donate button is a nice way to say thanks to the person who made one of the most influential artillery games ever. We deliberately don't mirror the bytes here — partly because ExeBrowser can't run them, and partly because the author's own page is simply the right place to get it.

sections.h: What you're missing (so you know it's worth the DOSBox detour)
sections.html:
Scorched Earth is a turn-based artillery duel for up to 10 tanks on a single 2D battlefield. You set angle and power, fire, and watch the terrain crater away. Between rounds you spend winnings in a shop on absurd ordnance — Funky Bombs, MIRVs, Napalm, Death's Heads, Tracers, parachutes, shields, and the gloriously dumb Riot Charges. It popularized destructible terrain and the "buy weapons between turns" loop that later defined Worms, Pocket Tanks, Gunbound and a thousand others.

It's a tiny, fast, mouse-and-keyboard game that runs perfectly under DOSBox on any modern machine. The only thing it can't do is run on Wine — and now you know exactly why.

faq.q: Can I play Scorched Earth online in my browser?
faq.a: Yes — but not through ExeBrowser/Wine. Scorched Earth is a DOS game, so it needs a DOS emulator. Browser builds at dos.zone and retrogames.cz run it in DOSBox-on-WebAssembly with no install.

faq.q: Why doesn't Scorched Earth work on ExeBrowser when other classics do?

faq.q: What's the difference between Scorched Earth and Worms?
faq.a: Scorched Earth (1991) came first and is a turn-based artillery game with stationary tanks, destructible terrain, and a between-rounds weapons shop. Worms (1995) took that template, added walking units and slapstick. If you like Worms, the DOS original is well worth a DOSBox session.

faq.a: Download scorch15.zip from the author, install DOSBox (or DOSBox-X), mount the game folder, and type SCORCH. Or just use a browser DOSBox build like dos.zone for zero setup.

intro: Jazz Jackrabbit — Epic MegaGames' blisteringly fast 1994 run-and-gun platformer, the one that made a gun-toting green rabbit a DOS-era icon. So the original JAZZ.EXE won't boot here. The good news: the open-source OpenJazz engine is a real Win32 program that loads the original Jazz data files and runs nicely in this player — so you can still play the free shareware levels in a browser tab.

sections.h: Why the original EXE won't run — and what does
sections.html:
Jazz Jackrabbit (1994) is a DOS game: it needs a 33 MHz 386, 4 MB of RAM, and runs in protected mode via a DOS extender. ExeBrowser's runtime is Boxedwine — Wine 1.7.55 plus a 32-bit x86 emulator — which executes Win32 and Win16 Windows programs. It is not a DOS emulator, so it can't load the original JAZZ.EXE. (For straight DOS play, a DOSBox-based site is the right tool.)

The practical route on ExeBrowser is OpenJazz (OpenJazz.exe): a faithful, GPL open-source re-implementation of the Jazz engine, shipped as a small 32-bit Windows executable. It doesn't include any game art or levels — you point it at the original Jazz data files (the shareware set is fine) and it plays them. That keeps everything legal: the engine is free software, and the level data is the shareware you already own.

sections.h: How to play it here
sections.html:
1. Get a Windows build of OpenJazz from its official releases page (GPL, free).
  2. Get the Jazz Jackrabbit shareware data files from a legitimate archive (or copy them from your GOG install). You want the level data files — the .000 / .j1 resources — plus the shareware levels.
  3. Put OpenJazz.exe and the Jazz data files together in one folder so the engine can find its data.
  4. Click ▶ Play above to boot Wine on this page, then use Load your own copy → a folder and select that folder.
  5. Pick OpenJazz.exe as the entry and click Run in Wine. Click the canvas to capture input.

This is a two-piece setup by design (free engine + your own data). It's a little more fiddly than a one-click game, but it's the honest, legal way to play Jazz in a browser.

sections.h: What to expect: speed, sound, controls
sections.html:
OpenJazz is light and 2D, so it tends to run at full speed in the player even though an x86 CPU is being emulated inside WebAssembly (roughly 10–40% of native, which is plenty for a 1994 platformer). Sound and music depend on the build and your browser, and the original tracker music may not always play cleanly under emulation — gameplay is the reliable part.

Default controls follow the classic layout:

  • ← → run, ↑ aim up / enter doors, ↓ crouch
  • Space or Alt jump · Ctrl fire
  • Enter confirm menus · Esc pause / release the mouse back to the page

If a particular OpenJazz build misbehaves, try the r18 Wine variant from the runtime menu before giving up.

faq.q: Can I play the original Jazz Jackrabbit EXE in the browser here?
faq.a: Not directly. The original 1994 game is a DOS program, and ExeBrowser runs Windows (Win32/Win16) binaries on Wine, not raw DOS. The realistic way to play on this site is the open-source OpenJazz engine, which is a Win32 executable that loads the original Jazz data files.

faq.q: Is Jazz Jackrabbit shareware free and legal to download?
faq.a: The shareware episode is widely distributed, but Epic's license is conditional — it must be labeled shareware (not freeware or public domain), Epic must be credited, the files must stay unaltered, and any distribution fee is capped. Because of those strings we don't host the files here; get them from a legitimate archive, or buy the full game on GOG.

faq.q: What is OpenJazz and do I still need the original game?
faq.a: OpenJazz is a free, GPL-licensed re-implementation of the Jazz Jackrabbit engine. It ships no game art or levels, so yes — you supply the original data files (the shareware set works). Engine free, data yours: that keeps it legal.

faq.q: Why is this rated 'partial' instead of 'works well'?
faq.a: Because the original DOS executable can't boot on a Win32 Wine runtime at all. You can play the shareware levels through OpenJazz, which works well, but it's a two-piece setup (free engine plus your own data) rather than a one-click launch of the real EXE — so 'partial' is the honest call.

faq.a: Gameplay is reliable; audio is hit-or-miss. The original tracker music and effects depend on the OpenJazz build and your browser, and may not always play cleanly through the emulator. Treat sound as a bonus, not a guarantee.

intro: One Must Fall 2097 — Epic MegaGames' 1994 robot-pilot fighting game, where Human-Assisted Robots (HARs) trade combos in a single-elimination tournament — is genuinely free freeware today, declared so by its developers in 1999. Great news. Here's the honest catch you won't get everywhere: OMF 2097 is a DOS game, not a Windows program. ExeBrowser runs Win32 .exe files on Wine + WebAssembly, and a raw DOS executable like OMF.EXE won't boot directly under Wine without a DOS layer. So the realistic ways to play it here are (1) through a Win32 build of DOSBox loaded into Wine, or (2) using OpenOMF, the open-source modern rebuild — both covered below. No piracy, no download required to read on.
licenseNote: One Must Fall 2097 is officially freeware but was a DOS game — it requires a DOS layer that Wine here does not support. Bring your own Win32 copy or source port above if you have one.

download.heading: Get One Must Fall 2097 (free, legal freeware)
download.html:
The original game — freeware since 1999

Diversions Entertainment and Epic MegaGames released OMF 2097 as freeware on 10 February 1999, with allowance for free non-commercial redistribution. The most reliable preserved copy is on the Internet Archive:

Get OMF 2097 freeware (Internet Archive) →

This is the DOS game (OMF.EXE + data files). It will not run as a plain Windows program under Wine on its own — pair it with a Win32 DOSBox build (see below) and point DOSBox at the OMF folder. © Epic MegaGames / Diversions Entertainment; redistributed under the developers' freeware terms.

Easier: OpenOMF (open-source rebuild)

If you just want to fight, OpenOMF is a free, open-source re-implementation of the engine. It still needs the original freeware data files (above) but is a modern build with windowed mode, gamepad and netplay support.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html: Be clear-eyed about this one. ExeBrowser's engine is Boxedwine — Wine 1.7.55 plus a 32-bit x86 emulator, which runs Windows 32-bit programs. OMF 2097 predates that world: it's a 1994 MS-DOS title that talks straight to DOS, VGA and a Sound Blaster, with no Windows API underneath. Hand Wine a raw DOS .exe and it generally won't start, because there's no DOS environment to host it. So the path to playing OMF here is to run a program that is Win32 and that, in turn, knows how to run DOS games: DOSBox.

sections.h: The realistic ways to play it here
sections.html:
1. DOSBox-in-Wine (emulation on emulation). Load a 32-bit Win32 build of DOSBox into ExeBrowser, then point it at your unzipped OMF folder and run OMF.EXE. This works in principle but stacks an x86 emulator (Boxedwine) under a DOS emulator (DOSBox), so expect it to be heavy and possibly slow — fine for a curiosity, not ideal for tight fighting-game inputs.
  2. OpenOMF (recommended for actually playing). The open-source rebuild is a real modern app with clean rendering and proper controls. Its native desktop builds are typically 64-bit, which ExeBrowser can't run — but it's the right pick on a normal desktop, and it only needs the freeware data files.
  3. OMF on the original DOS metal, elsewhere. On a real machine, DOSBox runs the freeware files perfectly at full speed. ExeBrowser shines for Win32 apps; for a pure DOS fighter, plain DOSBox on your desktop is the smoothest route.

Bottom line: the game is free and legal, and you can tinker with it in the browser, but it is not a one-click instant-play title the way a native Win32 game is. We'd rather tell you that than have it fail silently.

sections.h: About the game
sections.html: OMF 2097 is a one-on-one fighter set in 2097, where corporate-sponsored pilots climb a tournament ladder in giant Human-Assisted Robots. It's remembered for its tournament mode with upgradable robots and pilot stats — an RPG-ish layer rare for the genre — plus a thumping soundtrack and surprisingly deep move sets. It runs on a keyboard fine (the original was built for it), so you don't strictly need a gamepad.

faq.q: Is One Must Fall 2097 really free?
faq.a: Yes. Diversions Entertainment and Epic MegaGames declared it freeware on 10 February 1999, with allowance for free non-commercial redistribution. You can legally download and play the original game files at no cost.

faq.q: Can I play OMF 2097 in the browser with no download?

faq.q: Why won't One Must Fall 2097 just run in ExeBrowser like DOOM95?
faq.a: DOOM95 is a Windows 95 (Win32) port, which is exactly what Wine runs. OMF 2097 never got a Windows port — it's a pure MS-DOS game. Wine has no DOS environment, so it needs a DOS emulator like DOSBox (a Win32 program) in between, which adds overhead.

faq.q: What's the best way to actually play OMF 2097 today?
faq.a: On a normal desktop, run the freeware files in DOSBox (full speed), or use OpenOMF, the free open-source rebuild with windowed mode, gamepad and netplay. Both use the same legally-free data files. In the browser, DOSBox-in-Wine works but expect it to be slow.

faq.q: What is OpenOMF?
faq.a: OpenOMF is a free, open-source re-implementation of the OMF 2097 engine for modern systems. It plays the original freeware data files with modern conveniences like windowed/widescreen modes, gamepad support and TCP/IP multiplayer.

intro: Raptor: Call of the Shadows is Apogee's gloriously over-the-top 1994 vertical shoot-'em-up — buy bigger guns, blow up everything, repeat. It's a fantastic game, but it's an awkward fit for ExeBrowser, and we'd rather tell you straight than waste your time. The short version: the famous free shareware episode is a DOS game, and ExeBrowser runs Windows programs on Wine + WebAssembly, not DOS. The only Win32 version is a commercial DirectX 5 port that this old Wine engine handles poorly. So Raptor mostly belongs on a DOS emulator, not here — but read on for exactly what happens and the right way to play it.

sections.h: Why Raptor is a poor fit for ExeBrowser
sections.html:
ExeBrowser is a Windows runtime: it runs real Win32 .exe files inside Wine 1.7.55 on an emulated 32-bit x86 CPU. Raptor's problem is that its two relevant builds both fall outside that sweet spot:

  • The 1994 original (and the free shareware) is pure MS-DOS. It boots through the DOS4GW protected-mode extender and talks straight to VGA Mode-X hardware. That's a DOS program, not a Windows one — Wine has no DOS layer here, so it won't start. This is a job for a DOS emulator like DOSBox, not for Wine.
  • The 1999 Windows re-release is Win32, but built on DirectX 5. Boxedwine's bundled Wine is from the 1.7.55 era and its DirectDraw/Direct3D support is weak and incomplete, so DirectX games frequently show a black screen, garbled video, or simply refuse to start. On top of that, this version is commercial — we can't host it, and you'd need your own legally-bought copy.

That's why our verdict is partial at best. There's no free, Win32, Wine-friendly Raptor binary that lines up with all three constraints at once.

sections.h: The right way to actually play Raptor
sections.html:
Because the freely-shareable version is DOS, the best experience is a DOS emulator, not Wine:

  1. Play the shareware in a DOS-in-browser player. The Internet Archive and other preservation sites host the freely-redistributable Episode 1 running in an in-browser DOSBox/EM-DOSBOX emulator — that's purpose-built for DOS4GW games like Raptor and runs it cleanly.
  2. Buy the full game for modern systems. The Raptor 2015 Edition on Steam and the DRM-free editions on GOG bundle a configured emulator and run all three episodes (Bravo Sector, Tango Sector, Outer Regions) on Windows, often with cloud saves and achievements.

If you specifically want a Win32 shoot-'em-up that genuinely runs well in ExeBrowser, the shareware Apogee/Epic shmups built for Windows are a far better bet — see Tyrian and Jazz Jackrabbit below.

sections.h: If you want to try it here anyway
sections.html:
You can experiment — ExeBrowser lets you load your own folder — but go in with eyes open:

  • A DOS RAPTOR.EXE will not boot under Wine; expect it to bail out immediately or hang. This is expected, not a bug in your copy.
  • If you own the 1999 Windows port, you can load it via “Load your own copy” and try the default Win32 variant, then the r18 variant if it crashes. Be prepared for DirectX-related black screens or missing sound. We can't promise it'll get past the title screen.
  • There is also a fan-made open-source source port that re-implements the engine — but it still needs the original game's data files, so it doesn't change the licensing picture, and it isn't a drop-in Win32 build for this runtime.

None of this involves cracks or pirated copies — bring your own legally-owned files or stick to the free shareware on a proper DOS player.

faq.q: Can I play Raptor: Call of the Shadows free online with no download?
faq.a: Yes — but not here. The free shareware (Episode 1, "Bravo Sector") is a DOS game, so the no-download way to play it is an in-browser DOSBox player like the one on the Internet Archive. ExeBrowser runs Windows programs on Wine, not DOS games, so the shareware won't boot in this engine.

faq.a: Yes. Apogee released Episode 1 "Bravo Sector" as free shareware that may be copied and shared at no charge. The other two episodes (Tango Sector and Outer Regions) and the modern re-releases are commercial — buy those from Steam or GOG. Don't use cracked or "abandonware" copies of the paid content.

intro: Heretic (Raven Software, 1994) and Hexen: Beyond Heretic (1995) are the gothic-fantasy cousins of DOOM — same lineage, same fast 2.5D engine, but with spellcasting, inventory items, and looking up and down. Here's the honest part up front: the originals are DOS games, and ExeBrowser runs Win32 programs. The DOS .EXE will not boot here on its own. What does work is a small, free, modern Win32 source port — Chocolate Heretic or Chocolate Hexen — paired with the shareware/demo WAD. The engine is light software rendering with no DirectX, so a source-port EXE runs cleanly on Wine + WebAssembly. It's a two-piece setup (port + WAD), which is why we rate this partial rather than one-click.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html:
Heretic and Hexen are built on a derivative of the DOOM engine: software-rendered 2.5D, no hardware 3D, no DirectX. That's exactly the kind of workload ExeBrowser's emulated x86 + Wine handles well — frame rates are usually smooth on a modern laptop. The catch is the platform: the games id and Raven shipped in 1994–95 were DOS programs, and there was never an official Win32 build the way DOOM got DOOM95. Run the DOS HERETIC.EXE straight into Wine and it simply won't start.

The fix is a source port. Because Raven open-sourced the engine (GPL, 2008), projects like Chocolate Heretic and Chocolate Hexen rebuilt the original game as a tiny 32-bit Windows EXE that reads the original WAD data. Drop the port EXE and the WAD in one folder, load that folder here, and you get the real, faithful game running in the browser.

sections.h: What you need: the port plus a WAD
sections.html:
This is a two-file job. You supply both pieces; ExeBrowser supplies the runtime.

1. The engineA Win32 source port — Chocolate Heretic for Heretic, Chocolate Hexen for Hexen (free, GPL). Both are small 32-bit EXEs.
2. The game dataThe shareware/demo WAD: HERETIC1.WAD (Heretic shareware) or the Hexen demo WAD. You bring this from a legitimate source — see the box below.
Best Wine variantWin32 (default). If the port misbehaves, try the r18 engine.
Era / originDOS 1994–95; runs here via a modern Win32 source port, not the original EXE.
Expected resultSource port + shareware WAD: plays well. Original DOS EXE alone: will not start.

sections.h: Step by step
sections.html:
1. Download a Win32 build of Chocolate Heretic or Chocolate Hexen (the win32.zip from chocolate-doom.org) and unzip it.
  2. Obtain the shareware/demo WAD from a legitimate source (see the licensing box) and copy it into the same folder as the port EXE.
  3. Click ▶ Play above to boot Wine on this page.
  4. Use Load your own copy → a folder and select that folder so the EXE and WAD load together.
  5. Pick the source-port EXE as the entry and click Run in Wine. Click the canvas to capture input.

Heretic adds an inventory (Enter to use, [ and ] to cycle) and the ability to fly and look up/down — a step beyond DOOM's controls.

sections.h: Heretic vs. Hexen — and what's NOT included
sections.html:
Heretic shareware gives you the full first episode, City of the Damned — five levels of elf-vs-undead carnage with the iconic Wand and Phoenix Rod.

Hexen shipped a four-level demo (the start of the Seven Portals hub) rather than a classic shareware episode — it's a free demo, not a full free episode. It also introduced the class system (Fighter/Cleric/Mage) and hub-based puzzle progression.

The registered Heretic episodes, Heretic: Shadow of the Serpent Riders, the full Hexen, and the Deathkings expansion are all commercial — not included and not hosted here. The much later Hexen II is a different beast: it uses the Quake engine (true 3D, hardware acceleration) and is unlikely to run usefully under this 32-bit software-only setup.

faq.q: Can I play Heretic online in my browser for free?
faq.a: Yes, with two free pieces: a Win32 source port (Chocolate Heretic) plus the shareware WAD. The source port is free and GPL-licensed; you supply the shareware WAD from a legitimate source. ExeBrowser then runs the port in your browser via Wine + WebAssembly — nothing to install.

faq.q: Why won't the original Heretic or Hexen EXE run here?
faq.a: Because they're DOS programs, and ExeBrowser runs 32-bit Windows (Win32) programs. There was never an official Windows port the way DOOM got DOOM95. The practical route is a modern Win32 source port that reads the original WAD data — that's a real Windows EXE and runs cleanly.

faq.q: Is it legal to play the Heretic shareware and Hexen demo here?
faq.a: The source-port engine is open-source (GPL). The game data (WADs), however, is still copyrighted by Raven / id and was not given DOOM's explicit free-redistribution license, so we don't host the WADs. Bring your own from a legitimate source like the Internet Archive, or buy the full games on Steam. Avoid cracked or 'full version free' copies.

faq.q: Will Hexen II run in the browser?
faq.a: Almost certainly not usefully. Hexen II runs on the Quake engine — true 3D with hardware acceleration — which is far heavier than the DOOM-style software renderer Heretic and the first Hexen use. ExeBrowser has no DirectX/OpenGL acceleration, so a 3D Quake-engine game is a poor fit.

faq.q: How fast does it run?
faq.a: The engine is lightweight 2.5D software rendering with no DirectX, so once you've loaded a source port it typically plays smoothly on a modern laptop. ExeBrowser emulates an x86 CPU inside WebAssembly at roughly 10–40% of native speed, but DOOM-family games are light enough to feel fine; older Chromebooks and tablets may lag.

ogDescription: Blake Stone: Aliens of Gold shareware episode 1 — the 1993 Wolfenstein 3D-engine FPS — in a browser tab via the free, open-source BStone port and Wine + WebAssembly. Honest about what works.

author: Apogee Software

licenseReason: Episode 1 of Blake Stone: Aliens of Gold is officially free, freely-redistributable shareware (distributed by 3D Realms), and the BStone source port is open-source under GPL-2.0+, so both are legally hostable.
intro: Blake Stone: Aliens of Gold — Apogee's 1993 first-person shooter built on id Software's Wolfenstein 3D engine, with floor and ceiling textures, talking informants you could shoot (or spare) and six episodes of Dr. Goldfire's alien-infested labs. Episode 1 is free, freely-redistributable shareware, so it's completely legal to play here. One honest catch: the original is a DOS program, and ExeBrowser runs Win32 software, so the reliable way to play it in a tab is the free, open-source BStone source port loaded with the shareware data — not the original DOS executable.

sections.h: How it works & what to expect
sections.html:
ExeBrowser runs real 32-bit Windows executables on Wine + WebAssembly. The 1993 Blake Stone shipped as a DOS game (it wanted a 386 and ran in real/protected mode), and DOS binaries do not reliably boot under Wine in the browser — there's no DOS/V86 layer here. So pointing the player at the original BSTONE.EXE from the shareware archive will usually fail.

The practical route is BStone, the excellent open-source (GPL-2.0+) source port by Boris Bendovsky. It rebuilds the Blake Stone engine as a modern Win32/SDL2 program that reads the original shareware data files. Drop the BStone Windows build and the shareware data in one folder, load that folder, and you're running the actual game logic — just through a modern, browser-friendly executable.

EngineWolfenstein 3D engine (raycaster), via the BStone source port

sections.h: Why the verdict is "partial"
sections.html:
Two honest reasons, neither about the game being heavy:

  • The original is DOS. You can't just run the 1993 BSTONE.EXE here the way you'd run a Win32 app. A source port is required, which is an extra step.
  • The source port is modern SDL2. BStone is a clean, lightweight raycaster — exactly the kind of game that should run well at ExeBrowser's 10–40% of native speed — but modern SDL2 builds lean on Windows graphics and audio paths that are hit-or-miss under Boxedwine. It may launch and play smoothly, or it may stumble on video init or sound. That uncertainty is why this is "partial" rather than "works well."

The good news: the game itself is trivially light for a modern CPU, so if BStone initializes at all, gameplay won't be the bottleneck. Older 320×200-style render settings tend to be the most forgiving.

sections.h: Step by step
sections.html:
1. Grab the BStone Windows build (open-source, see the download box below) and the free shareware data for Aliens of Gold.
  2. Unzip both into a single folder so the BStone EXE sits next to the shareware data files.
  3. Click ▶ Play Blake Stone above, then use Load your own copy → a folder and pick that folder.
  4. Set the entry to the BStone executable (e.g. bstone.exe) and click Run in Wine.
  5. Click the canvas to capture input. ↑↓←→ move, Ctrl fires, Space opens doors and uses switches, 1–5 pick weapons, Esc releases the mouse.
  6. If it won't start on the default variant, retry with 18R2 and check the Console for err: lines.

faq.q: Is Blake Stone free to play?
faq.a: Episode 1 of Blake Stone: Aliens of Gold is free, officially-distributed shareware that may be redistributed unmodified at no charge (3D Realms hosts the v3.0 shareware episode). The remaining five episodes are the commercial registered version — buy those from a legitimate storefront. The BStone source port used to run it is free, open-source software under the GPL.

faq.q: Can I just run the original Blake Stone .exe in the browser?
faq.a: Usually not. The 1993 game is a DOS program, and ExeBrowser runs 32-bit Windows software via Wine — there's no DOS/V86 layer, so the original BSTONE.EXE typically won't boot. The reliable path is the BStone source port, a modern Win32 rebuild that reads the same shareware data.

faq.q: What is BStone and is it safe/legal?
faq.a: BStone is a free, open-source (GPL-2.0+) source port by Boris Bendovsky that re-implements the Blake Stone engine for Windows, Linux and macOS. It contains no game assets itself — it loads the data files from your shareware (or registered) copy. It's a legitimate, widely-used preservation project, not a crack or a pirated build.

faq.q: Does Blake Stone need DirectX or a 3D card?
faq.a: No. It's a Wolfenstein 3D-style raycaster from 1993 with no hardware-3D requirement, so the game is extremely light. The only real compatibility question here is whether the BStone port's SDL2 video and audio initialize cleanly under Boxedwine — the rendering itself is well within range.

faq.q: Why might Blake Stone not run well in ExeBrowser?
faq.a: Performance won't be the issue — the engine is tiny by modern standards. The risk is startup: a modern SDL2 source port can stumble on graphics or sound init inside the emulated environment. If the default Wine variant crashes on launch, try the 18R2 variant and watch the Console for missing-DLL or video errors.

description: Want to play the classic 1989/1990 Prince of Persia in your browser? Here's the honest story: the original is a commercial DOS game (now Ubisoft), so it won't run directly under Win32 Wine — but the free open-source SDLPoP reimplementation can.
keywords: play prince of persia online, prince of persia browser, prince of persia 1989 online, prince of persia dos in browser, sdlpop browser, classic prince of persia no download, prince of persia wine
ogTitle: Play Prince of Persia (Classic) Online — In Your Browser
ogDescription: The honest guide to running the classic Prince of Persia in a browser tab. The original is commercial DOS; the free open-source SDLPoP port is the realistic path. Here's what works.
h1: Play Prince of Persia (classic) in your browser
author: Brøderbund / Jordan Mechner

verdict.text: Partial — not the DOS original; the free SDLPoP port is the realistic path

licenseReason: The classic Prince of Persia is a commercial title (Brøderbund, now Ubisoft) and is not ours to redistribute; the open-source SDLPoP reimplementation is GPLv3 and free, but ExeBrowser does not host it for you.
licenseNote: Read this before you hunt for a download. The original Prince of Persia (1989 Apple II, 1990 MS-DOS) is a commercial game published by Brøderbund; the franchise is owned today by Ubisoft. Jordan Mechner released the original Apple II source code for study, but that grant explicitly does not give anyone the right to copy or distribute the game itself. So we do not host the game here. If you want the genuine article, buy the legitimate re-release — it is sold on GOG and Steam (search "Prince of Persia"). The free, legal way to play the classic gameplay is the open-source SDLPoP reimplementation described below. Please don't grab "abandonware" rips — the game is still being sold.
intro: Yes, you can relive the wall-running, sword-fighting, one-hour countdown of the original Prince of Persia in a browser — but let's be straight about how, because the obvious route doesn't work. The 1989/1990 classic is a DOS game, and ExeBrowser runs Win32 programs (Wine 1.7.55 on a WebAssembly x86 emulator). A raw DOS PRINCE.EXE has no Windows to talk to here, so it won't simply boot. The original is also a commercial Ubisoft-owned title, so it isn't ours to hand you. The realistic, honest, fully-legal path is SDLPoP — a faithful open-source (GPLv3) reimplementation of the DOS game that ships as a Win32 EXE and includes free game data. Below we explain exactly what to expect, and why even that comes with caveats.

sections.h: Why the original DOS game won't just run here
sections.html:
This trips a lot of people up, so here it is plainly. The classic Prince of Persia is an MS-DOS program from 1990. ExeBrowser's engine is Wine — it implements the Windows API, not DOS. When you hand Wine a 16-bit DOS executable like PRINCE.EXE, there is no DOS environment underneath it, so it typically refuses to start or exits immediately. That's not a bug in your file; it's simply the wrong kind of program for this tool.

The right tool for the unmodified DOS original is a DOS emulator such as DOSBox. ExeBrowser is for Windows .exe files. So if your goal is the byte-for-byte 1990 release, you want a DOSBox-based setup (and a legally purchased copy from GOG, where it's pre-packaged with DOSBox), not this page.

sections.h: The realistic path: SDLPoP, the open-source reimplementation
sections.html:
SDLPoP is a beloved, actively-maintained, open-source (GPLv3) port built from a disassembly of the DOS game, with Jordan Mechner's blessing for the community effort. It plays exactly like the classic — same levels, same physics, same 60-minute clock — but as a modern, cross-platform program. Crucially for legality, the standard SDLPoP release bundles free game data, so it runs standalone without any commercial files. It ships a Windows build (prince.exe), which means it's a Win32 program ExeBrowser can attempt to run.

We don't host it for you (it's not our binary to redistribute pre-configured, and it updates often), but it's a one-click download from the project. Grab the Windows release, unzip the whole folder (the EXE needs its data/ directory and SDL DLLs beside it), then use “Load your own copy → a folder” in the player above and pick that unzipped folder.

sections.h: What to expect — and the honest caveat
sections.html:
Here's where we won't overpromise. SDLPoP is built on SDL and leans on hardware-accelerated rendering. Boxedwine's graphics support is limited and software-driven, so SDL-based games are hit-or-miss: some boot to a playable (if slow) window, others throw a renderer error or show a black screen. Combine that with the x86-on-WebAssembly speed penalty (~10–40% of native) and you may get choppy frames. The good news is that Prince of Persia is a tiny, low-resolution game, so when it does render it often stays playable.

If SDLPoP refuses to start, it's worth trying the r18 Wine variant (an older Boxedwine engine that sometimes succeeds where the default crashes) before giving up. There's no guarantee — this is firmly a “try it and see” title, which is why we mark it partial rather than promising it works.

What you actually runSDLPoP (open-source Win32 build), not the commercial DOS original
Best Wine variantdefault; if it crashes, try r18
Game dataSDLPoP ships free data — no commercial files needed
Expected resultHit-or-miss: SDL rendering may or may not initialize under Boxedwine
The DOS originalWon't run here (it's DOS, not Win32); use DOSBox + a legit copy

sections.h: Step by step (SDLPoP)
sections.html:
1. Download the latest Windows release from the SDLPoP releases page.
  2. Unzip the whole archive on your computer — keep prince.exe, the data/ folder, and the SDL DLLs together in one folder.
  3. Click ▶ Play above, then choose Load your own copy → a folder and select that unzipped SDLPoP folder.
  4. The player will pick prince.exe as the entry point; click Run in Wine.
  5. If it boots: arrow keys move, Shift grabs ledges / draws sword, ↑ jumps. If it errors or shows black, retry with the r18 variant.

faq.q: Can I play the original Prince of Persia online for free with no download?
faq.a: Not the genuine 1990 DOS original — that's a commercial Ubisoft-owned game, and it's a DOS program that doesn't run under ExeBrowser's Windows engine anyway. What you can play for free and legally is SDLPoP, an open-source reimplementation that includes free game data. You download SDLPoP (one click) and load its folder here; whether it renders depends on Boxedwine's limited SDL support.

faq.q: Why won't the DOS PRINCE.EXE start in the browser here?
faq.a: Because it's a DOS executable, not a Windows one. ExeBrowser runs Wine, which implements the Windows API — there's no DOS layer underneath, so a raw DOS game like the original Prince of Persia has nothing to run against. For the unmodified DOS original you'd use a DOSBox-based setup instead.

faq.q: Is it legal to play Prince of Persia on ExeBrowser?
faq.a: Playing the free, open-source SDLPoP port is completely legal — it's GPLv3 and ships its own free data. The original commercial game is owned by Ubisoft and isn't free to copy; we don't host it, and we'd steer you to buy it on GOG or Steam rather than grab an abandonware rip.

faq.q: Didn't Jordan Mechner release the source code? Doesn't that make it free?
faq.a: Mechner released the original Apple II source code for people to study and learn from — and that's wonderful — but he was explicit that it does not grant any rights to make or distribute Prince of Persia itself; that's Ubisoft's franchise. The community port SDLPoP is the freely-distributable way to actually play, because it's an independent GPLv3 codebase with its own free data.

faq.q: Will SDLPoP definitely work in ExeBrowser?
faq.a: No guarantees, which is why this page is marked partial. SDLPoP uses SDL and hardware rendering, and Boxedwine's graphics support is limited and software-only, so it may launch fine, run slowly, or fail to initialize the display. It's worth trying the default variant first, then the r18 variant if that crashes. The game itself is tiny, so when it renders it's often playable.
